title: 2018 Census data products to be released from 23 September | Stats NZ
content:
## 2018 Census data products to be released from 23 September
04 September 2019, 10:45am
A range of products and other services are being prepared in anticipation of the release of 2018 Census data on 23 September 2019.
- 2018 Census releases for 23 September
- Information by variable and external data quality panel reports
- Upcoming methods papers
- Pre-release seminars
The key dates to keep in mind are:
- 9–18 September: pre-release seminars will be held in the main centres
- 23 September: the first releases containing 2018 Census data published
- 23 September: external data quality panel’s initial report, and definitions in DataInfo+ published
- from 23 September: various methods papers published
- December: external data quality panel’s second report published.

### 2018 Census releases for 23 September
Three releases of 2018 Census data will be published on 23 September:
- Number of electorates and electoral populations: 2018 Census – this will support the electoral boundary-setting process ahead of the 2020 General Election
- 2018 Census population and dwelling counts – includes census usually resident population counts at national (New Zealand) and sub-national levels, and census night population counts
- 2018 Census totals by topic – this includes tables with data for over 60 topics, see List of topics .
The population counts (above) will be broken down by age, sex, and ethnic groups, which is a new development. Previously, population counts were only provided by geography.
From 23 September, we will start processing prioritised customised data requests. See Customised data services for more about this service.
After the first data is published we will release more information, progressively, up until June 2020.
These future releases will include commentary, tables, graphs, and sometimes interactive maps, to help explain the data for different customer segments. The topics and services will include:
- place summaries – standardised information about a geographic area, including regional councils, territorial authorities, and statistical area 2 (SA2s)
- housing – including housing quality (heating, damp, facilities) and tenure
- ethnicity, culture and identity – including country of birth, languages, and religions
- microdata (de-identified data about units of a population, such as individuals or households) – for approved qualified researchers to access for research or statistical purposes in the public interest (no information about individual people or households will be published or disseminated) (see Integrated data ):
- 2018 Census data will also be linked to the Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI) as a priority product given the demand for updated and new time series information in the IDI
- standard datasets – all census variables, including variables that relate to how the data is processed and linked
- open data API – for registered users to send coded requests for specific variables from a dataset
- population and migration – including population characteristics, and internal migration
- Māori – ethnicity and descent with information such as age, and te reo
- travel to work and education – including an interactive tool showing the number of people moving between areas
- SA1 dataset – data from the smallest geographic output area (SA1) for a wide range of census variables
- activity limitations – by age, sex and ethnicity
- ethnic group profiles – standardised information covering a range of topics for selected ethnic groups
- smoking – including cigarette smoking behaviour by age, sex, and ethnicity
- education and training – including qualifications and participation in study
- work, income, and unpaid activities – including occupation, employment, unpaid activities, and income
- population and migration (other topics) – including number of children born, partnership and relationship status.

### Information by variable and external data quality panel reports
On 23 September, information by variable and census definitions will be available on DataInfo+ . At the same time, the external data quality panel will publish the first of two reports. The first report will focus on the census data released so far and the second report (to be published in December 2019) will discuss data quality. 2018 Census external data quality panel: Terms of reference has more information about the external data quality panel.
As we explained in July, the majority of key variables are of very high, high, or moderate quality, but some are poor or very poor (see Customer update on data quality of 2018 Census ).
Three key variables were identified as being very poor quality:
- Families and households – we are investigating whether we can improve the information for families and households. It will not be available on the 23 September, but we will provide a further update at that time.
- Iwi affiliation – we are continuing to work with the Data Iwi Leaders Group to consider the quality of data and identify possible solutions to the data gap.
- Absentees – the new methodology used to create the census dataset has reduced the importance of this variable. Absentees are not used for population counts. The main impact of poor absentees data is on the quality of families/households data.

### Upcoming methods papers
We will be publishing a wide range of papers to help customers understand the census process that was implemented and the new methods used to create the census dataset. The first suite of papers covering the following topics will be available from early September:
- developing the forms
- creating the census dwelling frame
- communication, marketing, and engagement strategies
- targeted field strategies
- processing and evaluating the 2018 Census data.
Further technical papers detailing the methods used to create the census dataset will be available from 23 September.
Quick guide to the 2018 Census explains what was different about the 2018 Census and key changes.

### Pre-release seminars
Pre-release seminars will be held across five main centres (Auckland, Hamilton, Wellington, Christchurch, and Dunedin) between 9 and 18 September. The seminars will help customers interpret and use 2018 Census data and will focus on the release of 2018 Census data, data quality and assurance, and metadata. Although the seminars will be tailored for key customers, they will include information that will benefit a wide range of census customers. The information will be relevant to central and local government, researchers, academics, business professionals, and high-level community users of census data. A webinar session will be available on 17 September.
If you would like more information on the seminars or would like to attend, please contact info@stats.govt.nz .
The following census totals by topics will be in the data release on 23 September: Activity limitations (total responses) Age (single years) Birthplace Cigarette smoking behaviour Ethnic group (total responses) Highest qualification Highest secondary school qualification Hours worked in employment per week Hours worked per week in main job Hours worked per week in other jobs Individual home ownership Industry (ANZSIC06 V1.0) Job search methods (total responses) Language spoken (total responses) Legally registered relationship status Main means of travel to education Main means of travel to work Māori descent indicator Number of children born Occupation (ANZSCO V1.2) Official language indicator Partnership status in current relationship Post-school qualification field of study Post-school qualification in New Zealand indicator Post-school qualification level of attainment Religious affiliation (total responses) Sector of ownership Sex Sources of personal income (total responses) Status in employment Study participation Total personal income Unpaid activities (total responses) Usual residence one year ago indicator Usual residence five years ago indicator Usual residence indicator Work and labour force status Workplace address indicator Years at usual residence Years since arrival in New Zealand Access to basic amenities (total responses) Access to telecommunication systems (total responses) Dwelling dampness indicator Dwelling mould indicator Dwelling occupancy status Dwelling type Fuel types used to heat dwellings (total responses) Main types of heating (total responses) Number of bedrooms Number of census night occupants Number of motor vehicles Number of rooms Sector of landlord Tenure of household Weekly rent paid by household for households
